actions.thought: Ten-Six offsuit is a weak hand even in a three-handed game from the button. While it's possible to open wide in this position, T6o doesn't have good playability or value. Since it's the first hand of the tournament, I'll play conservatively and fold this marginal hand to wait for better spots.

3d 2c is an extremely weak starting hand with very little playability. Even though I have already posted 50 as the small blind, continuing to play this hand out of position against the big blind would be a negative expected value move. Folding is the most disciplined and standard choice to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am on the button with Jack-Five suited, which is a playable hand in a three-handed game. Opening the pot from this position is the optimal strategy to either win the blinds immediately or play a hand with the positional advantage. A raise to 250, which is 2.5 times the big blind, is a standard size to test the opponents' resistance.
K9o is too weak to play from the small blind against a button raise. Being out of position with a marginal offsuit hand is a recipe for losing chips, so folding is the disciplined and correct play.

I am in the Big Blind with 9s 3s. The button (Flashlight) has raised to 250, and the Small Blind folded. I need to call 150 more into a pot of 400. While I'm getting decent pot odds (approximately 27%) and the hand is suited, 93s is a very weak hand that plays poorly out of position. In a 3-handed game, ranges are wider, but defending such a weak hand against a raise from the button often leads to difficult post-flop situations where equity realization is low. I will choose to fold and preserve my stack for better opportunities.

J7 offsuit is a weak hand to open from the button, even three-handed. It lacks the playability and equity to comfortably steal the blinds and continue post-flop against resistance. Folding is the optimal decision to preserve chips.

In the Small Blind with J7o after the Button folded, I am facing only the Big Blind. While ranges are wider in blind-vs-blind situations, J7o is a weak hand that does not play well post-flop, especially since I will be out of position for the remainder of the hand. Given it is very early in the tournament (Hand 3 of 150), there is no need to take an unnecessary risk or play a marginal hand. I will choose to fold and wait for better opportunities.

I am on the button with Ace-Queen offsuit, which is a premium hand in a 3-handed game. Standard strategy dictates a raise here to build value and take control of the pot. I will open for 300 chips (3x the big blind).

73s is an extremely weak hand and playing it out of position in the small blind against a button open is a losing proposition. Even with the pot odds, defending such a hand often leads to difficult post-flop situations where I am likely behind. Folding is the disciplined and correct decision this early in the tournament.

Facing a Button raise, A5 suited is a strong hand to defend from the Big Blind. It has excellent post-flop playability with nut flush draw potential, wheel straight possibilities, and top pair value. Since I am out of position, flat calling is a standard and safe line to see the flop while keeping the pot manageable.

Being out of position as the pre-flop caller on a dry paired board (9c 3d 9s), the standard play is to check to the pre-flop raiser. Leading out (donk betting) here doesn't accomplish much, as we don't represent a 9 well, and we want to see what our opponent does. With Ace-high and some backdoor potential, we can evaluate our options depending on the size of their continuation bet.

I have Ace-Queen high on a dry, paired board (9-9-3). While I haven't improved, my hand is likely the best one currently. Pro has checked to me after calling my pre-flop raise. I will make a small continuation bet of 225 chips, which is approximately one-third of the pot. This sizing is standard on paired boards to put pressure on the opponent's 'air' hands while keeping the pot manageable if they have a piece of the board. If I am raised, I can comfortably fold, and if called, I still have two overcards to potentially improve on later streets.

On a dry paired board like 9-3-9, the pre-flop raiser will frequently continuation bet with their entire range. While our Ace-high may occasionally be the best hand right now, we are out of position, have very weak backdoor draws, and will face tough decisions on the turn and river. Calling a c-bet here with just A5 high without strong potential to improve is unprofitable in the long run. Folding is the disciplined choice.

7-10 offsuit is a marginal hand even on the button in a 3-handed game. With 100BB stacks, it is better to conserve chips and wait for a higher equity starting hand rather than contesting pots with weak cards early in the tournament.
J4o is a very weak hand and playing it out of position in the small blind, even for half a blind, is a losing play. I'll fold and wait for better spots.
I am dealt 3s 2s on the button in a 3-handed game. This hand is too weak to play, even in late position. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

With 8d 4s in the Small Blind after the Button has folded, I am looking at a very weak starting hand. While I only need to put in 50 more to see a flop, 8-high offsuit plays very poorly out of position and is easily dominated. Folding is the standard disciplined play here to conserve chips early in the tournament.

I am on the button with 73o, which is a very weak hand. While the button is an ideal position for stealing blinds, 73o has almost no playability or showdown value if called. Given it is early in the tournament, I will stick to a solid range and fold this trash hand.

With pocket nines in the small blind after the button folds, I have a strong equity advantage heads-up against the big blind. Raising is the optimal play to capitalize on this strength, take initiative, and build a pot, rather than letting the big blind see a free flop.

Facing a 3x raise from the Small Blind, A4o is a weak, easily dominated hand. Even though we would have position post-flop, A4o struggles to realize its equity and rarely flops strong enough to play a large pot. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here to preserve chips.

Holding Queen-Nine offsuit on the button is a standard hand to open, as it offers sufficient strength to potentially take down the blinds or play in position. With a stack of 10,250 and blinds at 50/100, opening to 250 is a standard sizing to balance opening ranges and put pressure on the blinds.

KhJh is a very strong hand in a 3-handed game, especially facing a Button open. While calling is an option, it risks allowing the Big Blind to squeeze or overcall, forcing me to play multi-way out of position. 3-betting seizes the initiative, extracts value from a wide Button opening range, and forces the opponents into a tough decision.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Nine offsuit. The Button (Flashlight) opened for 2.5x, and the Small Blind (Pro) 3-bet to 850. Queen-Nine offsuit is significantly behind a standard 3-betting range from the Small Blind, especially when facing action from the Button as well. Calling 750 more into a 1200 pot out of position against two opponents is a losing play in the long run. Folding is the most prudent action to preserve my stack.

Facing a 3-bet from the small blind with Queen-Nine offsuit is unfavorable. While I have position on the button, this hand is dominated by the standard 3-bet range of an opponent (which typically includes premium pairs and strong Ace-X hands). Calling would require committing significant chips (600 more) to a pot where I am likely behind, and playing a bloated pot with a mediocre hand is negative expectation. Folding is the most disciplined play here to preserve my stack.
Holding J2 suited on the button 3-handed. This is a very weak starting hand, well outside of a standard opening range. The best and safest play is to fold.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Six suited. Since the Button folded, it is only me and the Big Blind left in the hand. Ace-Six suited is a strong starting hand in a short-handed situation. I will raise to 300 to exert pressure on the Big Blind and potentially win the pot immediately or play a strong hand with initiative post-flop.

Ace-Four offsuit is a defendable hand in the Big Blind against a Small Blind raise. Since the button folded, I have position for the remainder of the hand. The pot odds are favorable (calling 200 to win 400), making a call the most strategically sound decision to see a flop without overcommitting with a marginal hand.

The flop of 7d-9h-7c is a paired board that generally favors the pre-flop aggressor. Although I only have Ace-high, I maintain the initiative. A continuation bet here is standard to represent a strong overpair or even a piece of the board, forcing the Big Blind to fold their air and weaker hands. I will bet 250, slightly less than half the pot, to maintain pressure while keeping the price reasonable.

The flop of 7d-9h-7c is a paired board that strongly hits the range of the aggressor, who likely has a strong range or at least a high pair/trips. My hand, Ace-high (As4h), has almost no showdown value and very little equity on this texture. Calling here would be a marginal play with low potential to improve, and against a c-bet on a paired board, folding is the most disciplined and strategically sound decision to preserve my stack.

9-5 offsuit is a very weak hand, even for a button open in a three-handed game. There is no need to force a steal with such low-equity cards when the stacks are deep. I will fold and wait for a more favorable situation.

With the button having folded, I am in a heads-up situation in the small blind against the big blind. King-seven offsuit is a reasonable hand to play aggressively in this position. By raising to 300, I can exert pressure on the big blind, aiming to either win the pot immediately or enter the hand with the initiative.

Pocket tens is a premium starting hand, especially blind versus blind. Flashlight raised from the Small Blind, indicating their range could be relatively wide. I will 3-bet to 900 for value to extract chips from weaker pairs and broadway hands, and to take control of the pot with a strong positional advantage post-flop.

Holding King-seven offsuit in the small blind, facing a 3-bet from the big blind, is a disadvantageous spot. Being out of position with a weak holding against a likely value-heavy 3-betting range makes calling unprofitable, as I would be dominated by many of the opponent's holdings. Folding is the disciplined choice here to preserve my stack.

T-3 offsuit is an extremely weak starting hand with very little equity. Even with the positional advantage of being on the button, there is no merit to playing this hand against two opponents who likely hold stronger ranges. Folding is the most disciplined and optimal play here to preserve my stack for a more profitable opportunity.

Playing K5o from the Small Blind against the Big Blind is generally unprofitable because we will be out of position post-flop with a weak, disconnected hand. Folding is the standard and safest play here.

Pocket sevens is a strong starting hand, especially three-handed from the button. Opening with a raise to 2.5x the big blind (250) will thin the field, allow me to take down the blinds uncontested often, or play a decent hand in position post-flop.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Trey offsuit, which is a very weak hand. Pro has opened from the Button to 2.5x the big blind. Playing out of position with such a weak hand against a Button raise is generally unprofitable. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

The opponent opened from the button to 2.5x, which is a standard sizing. I am in the big blind with King-three offsuit. While this hand is relatively weak, the pot odds (150 to call into a 400 pot) make it profitable to defend against a wide button opening range. Folding would be too passive given the odds, so I will call and see a flop.

I am in the big blind with King-high on a board of 9h-4c-6c. I have no pair and no significant draw, so I have no reason to lead out with a bet. Checking is the standard play to evaluate the opponent's action and control the size of the pot.

Our pocket sevens are currently second pair on this 9-high coordinated board. We have an underpair to the 9 but an overpair to the 4 and 6, along with a gutshot straight draw and a club for backdoor flush potential. Betting here helps deny equity to overcards (like broadway hands) and extracts value from straight/flush draws or smaller pairs. I'll bet a little under half-pot to protect my hand and charge draws.

The flop of 9-4-6 is quite dry but connects well with a button opening range, which likely includes many overpairs, nines, and middle cards. With only King-high and no meaningful draw (just a weak backdoor flush draw), I have almost no equity to improve to the best hand against a standard continuation bet. Calling 250 into a 800 pot with such a weak hand is mathematically incorrect and would lead to difficult decisions on later streets. Folding is the most disciplined and profitable play here to conserve my stack.

Holding King-six offsuit on the button in a three-handed game, I have a standard opening hand. My position allows me to put pressure on the blinds. Although Pro in the big blind has shown some 3-betting aggression in previous hands, folding this hand on the button would be too tight. I will raise to 2.5x the big blind to try and take the pot pre-flop or play the hand in position if called.

K2o is a weak hand, and being out of position against the button makes it even less playable. With the big blind yet to act and no incentive to defend such a marginal holding, folding is the disciplined choice to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

4s6s is a suited one-gapper, which is quite weak to play out of position against a Button open. While we are getting somewhat decent odds, playing this hand out of position often leads to marginal situations. Folding is the safest and standard play here to preserve our stack.

Pocket Tens are a strong hand, especially in position on the button. Raising to 3bb (300) is a standard open to thin the field and build value pre-flop. This allows me to define my range and take control of the hand.

78s is a great suited connector that serves as an excellent 3-bet bluff candidate from the Small Blind. Facing a 3x Button open, calling is suboptimal due to being out of position and inviting a Big Blind squeeze. A 3-bet to 950 puts pressure on Flashlight's wider Button opening range, allows us to win the pot uncontested pre-flop, and gives us excellent playability if we are called.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Eight offsuit. Flashlight opened from the button and Pro 3-bet from the Small Blind. My hand is very weak and does not play well against a 3-betting range, especially out of position. Folding is the only sensible move here to preserve my stack.

Pocket Tens are a strong hand that plays well in position, especially against a 3-bet from the Small Blind. With a stack depth of 88bb, calling is the most balanced approach to realize equity post-flop, as a 4-bet would likely only be called by hands that currently dominate me (JJ+) or force folds from hands I am currently leading (like 88, 99, or AQ). By calling, I maintain control of the pot size while keeping the opponent's range wide.

On a dry, paired flop of 2d 3d 3h, my pre-flop 3-betting range maintains a significant advantage. My range contains many overpairs (AA-TT) and strong broadway combinations. I flopped a flush draw with 7d8d, giving me excellent equity to semi-bluff. A small continuation bet of around 30% of the pot keeps the initiative, puts pressure on Flashlight's overcards and floats, and builds the pot for when my flush hits. It also allows me to balance my value c-bets effectively.

The flop of 2d-3d-3h is extremely safe for my pocket Tens. My opponent's small bet of 600 into a 2600 pot (roughly 23% pot) suggests they are either testing the waters with a marginal hand or under-representing a larger overpair. Since I have an overpair and there are no flush or straight draws that threaten my hand significantly, calling is the optimal play to keep the opponent's wider range in the hand and control the pot size. Raising here might only isolate myself against a stronger range that I currently fear, while calling allows me to evaluate the turn based on their next move.

The 6c turn is a blank that doesn't change the board texture, keeping my perceived range of overpairs (AA-TT) firmly in the lead. Flashlight's flop calling range against a small continuation bet largely consists of floating overcards (AK, AQ) and medium pocket pairs (44-99) that will struggle against further aggression. Firing a second barrel of half the pot applies maximum pressure on these marginal hands to fold. Additionally, I still have 9 outs to the diamond flush, giving me strong equity to improve to the winning hand on the river if called.

The board (2d-3d-3h-6c) is very dry, and my pocket Tens remain an overpair to everything except for an unlikely trips holding in the opponent's range. The opponent's bet of 1600 into a 3200 pot gives me 3:1 pot odds, making this an easy call. Raising would not serve a clear purpose, as it would likely isolate me against hands that have me beat while forcing folds from the wide range of bluffs or weaker pairs that the opponent could be betting with. Calling keeps the pot manageable and allows me to re-evaluate on the river.

The Qc on the river is a double-edged sword; it connects well with my perceived 3-betting range (such as AQ and KQ) but also improves any AQ floats my opponent might have been holding. Having completely missed my flush draw, I am left with 8-high and zero showdown value, meaning I can only win the pot by bluffing. However, my specific holding of the 7d and 8d makes this a poor bluffing candidate. By holding two diamonds, I block the missed diamond flush draws that make up a substantial portion of my opponent's natural folding range. Because I block his likely folds, his range is more heavily weighted toward made hands that will call an all-in, such as medium pocket pairs (which might stubbornly call), a newly hit top pair (AQ), or slowplayed monsters. Attempting a triple-barrel all-in bluff here is unnecessarily risky given the negative blocker effects. Checking and conceding the pot is the most disciplined and mathematically sound decision, preserving a healthy 72 BB stack for more favorable spots.

The opponent checked the river after betting the flop and turn, suggesting they might have missed a draw or are potentially trapping with a strong hand. However, my pocket Tens have significant showdown value against a wide range of bluffs or lower pairs. Since I am unlikely to be called by worse hands if I bet, checking behind is the optimal play to realize my equity and avoid getting check-raised or value-bet against a range that beats me.

Pre-flop on the Button with Jack-Six offsuit. This is a very weak hand with extremely low equity and poor playability. Standard strategy dictates folding this hand, as attempting to steal the blinds with it is generally unprofitable, especially against opponents who have shown a willingness to play back.

I am in the Small Blind with Qd9d, a strong hand for a blind-vs-blind battle after the Button has folded. Q9s has significant equity and playability post-flop. I will raise to 300 to put pressure on the Big Blind and take control of the pot early. Standard strategy in this position is to open a wide range, and Q9s is well within the top tier of that range.

Flash raised from the small blind and Pro folded. Being in the big blind with Queen-Nine offsuit, I have sufficient pot odds to defend. Given that the raise size is relatively standard, folding would be too exploitable. I will call to see a flop in position.

As the pre-flop aggressor, I have a significant range advantage on this Ace-high board. Most of my opening range from the Small Blind contains high cards and Aces that the Big Blind would have likely 3-bet. A standard continuation bet of 250 (roughly 42% of the pot) allows me to represent the Ace and put pressure on the Big Blind's mid-pair or air hands, forcing them to fold equity. If called, I will evaluate the turn based on whether a heart or a high diamond appears.

The flop of Ace-High, Four-Heart, Seven-Spade completely misses my hand of Queen-Nine offsuit. The board texture is quite dry, but an Ace is squarely in the pre-flop aggressor's range. With no pair, no draw, and no backdoor equity worth pursuing, there is no justification for calling a bet here. Folding preserves my stack for a more advantageous situation.

I am on the button with Ten-Six offsuit. While it is a three-handed game, this hand is quite weak and doesn't play well post-flop. Both opponents in the blinds, Flashlight and Pro, have shown significant aggression in previous hands, frequently raising and re-raising. Opening here with a marginal hand risks a 3-bet or being forced to play a bloated pot out of position or against a stronger range. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

With 9-7 offsuit, my hand is too weak to justify playing out-of-position against the Big Blind. While stealing blinds is a standard tactic, this specific holding lacks the equity to contest the pot, and I prefer to preserve my stack rather than engage in a marginal spot against a reasonably competent opponent.

I am on the button with Q9 offsuit, which is a playable hand for an open-raise in a three-handed game. I want to put pressure on the blinds to win the pot pre-flop or gain positional advantage, so a standard open-raise of 2.5x the big blind is appropriate.

K7o is a very weak hand to play from the small blind against a button raise. It is easily dominated, has poor playability post-flop out of position, and the big blind still has to act. Folding is the standard and correct decision here.

I am in the Big Blind with 95 offsuit. Flashlight opened from the button for 2.5x. While the pot odds are approximately 2.7:1 to call, 95 offsuit is a very weak hand that plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position. Given the stack sizes and the tournament stage, there is no reason to defend with such a marginal hand.
With Qc 8c on the Button in a 3-handed game, this is a standard open-raise to steal the blinds. The hand has decent playability post-flop if called, and we can often win the pot uncontested.

T5s is a marginal suited hand. Calling a button raise from the small blind puts me in a difficult situation, playing out of position against 'Pro' who has shown aggressive tendencies. To maintain a healthy stack and avoid high-variance spots with weak hands, folding is the standard and most disciplined play here.

I am in the big blind with Jack-Seven suited, which is a standard hand to defend against a button open. Given the pot odds (150 to call into a total pot of 400), it is mathematically profitable to defend here and see a flop.

I am in the big blind holding a flush draw with Jack-high on an Ace-high board. Since I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, the standard strategy is to check and see how the button reacts to the board texture. This allows me to control the pot and re-evaluate based on their bet size.

The A-3-5 flop heavily favors my pre-flop raising range, as I have all the strong Aces. Although I missed the flop with Qc 8c, this is a very strong candidate for a small continuation bet as a bluff. A bet of roughly 1/3 pot will fold out the Big Blind's random unpaired cards and some weak pairs, allowing me to win the pot uncontested frequently. My backdoor club draw also gives me some potential barrel opportunities on the turn.

I am holding a flush draw on an Ace-high board. The opponent has made a small continuation bet of 200 into a 550 pot, giving me favorable pot odds to call. With 9 outs for the flush, my immediate equity is approximately 19%, which justifies a call. Given my stack size and the relative strength of a flush draw, playing passively by calling is the most standard and effective way to realize my equity and potentially outplay the opponent if I hit.

The turn card, the nine of spades, has completed my flush. Given that the pot is 950 and I am currently in a strong position, I need to extract value from the opponent who has been the aggressor pre-flop and on the flop. Betting here is superior to checking because it denies a free card if the opponent has an Ace or a smaller spade, and helps build the pot for a larger river bet.

The turn card completes the obvious spade flush draw, and Flashlight leads out with a bet of 500. I hold Queen high with no spades and no viable draw to improve. With absolutely no equity against a made hand and no strong bluffing candidates holding the Qc 8c, the only correct action is to fold.

I am on the button with a premium hand, Queen of Clubs and Queen of Hearts. In a three-handed game, this is an extremely strong starting hand. I want to build the pot immediately and gain control of the betting. Standard opening raises in this game have been around 2.5x to 3x the big blind, so I will raise to 300.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Jack offsuit, which is a strong hand in a three-handed game. Calling out of position against the button is generally weak as it concedes the initiative. I will 3-bet to 950 to take control of the hand, bloat the pot, and capitalize on my position relative to the button's frequent opening range.

Facing an open from the Button and a 3-bet from the Small Blind, JTo is completely unplayable. The hand is too weak to call a 3-bet, especially with the original raiser still left to act behind me. Folding to preserve my stack is the only viable option.

I have Pocket Queens, which is a premium hand in any poker format and especially strong in 3-handed play. After my initial raise to 300, Flashlight has 3-bet to 950. I should not just call here, as I want to build the pot and take control of the betting lead. A 4-bet allows me to get value from hands like JJ, TT, AK, and AQ, while also making it difficult for my opponent to realize their equity if they have overcards. I will raise to 2,400 to put the pressure back on Flashlight.

Facing a 4-bet from the button, my Ace-Jack offsuit is a bit thin to continue, but given the pot odds and the opponent's aggressive history in this session, folding immediately feels too exploitable. I will make the call to see the flop, keeping the pot manageable, and play cautiously from out of position given the 4-bet strength.

I am out of position against an opponent who showed significant strength by 4-betting pre-flop. With top pair and a good kicker on a relatively dry board, checking is the most prudent approach to keep the pot size manageable and induce potential bluffs from the button. Leading out would commit me to a pot where I may be behind a range containing overpairs or a better jack.

I have an overpair (QQ) on a Jack-high flop (3d Js 6s). Given that I 4-bet pre-flop and the stack-to-pot ratio (SPR) is very low (around 1.2), I am committed to this hand. Flashlight's check is expected. I need to bet for value against hands like AJ, KJ, TT, and flush draws. A bet of 1,800 (approximately 37% of the pot) builds the pot for a potential turn shove while still allowing Flashlight to call with a wider range of second-best hands.

Facing a 4-bet pre-flop, the opponent's range is polarized but heavily weighted towards strong cards like AK, AQ, or overpairs. However, the flop of 3d-Js-6s is relatively dry and misses much of that 4-betting range. My top pair, Jack kicker, is likely ahead of air and potentially AK/AQ that is continuation-betting. The bet of 1800 into a pot of approximately 4900 is small, offering good pot odds to call and re-evaluate on the turn. Folding top pair here would be overly cautious against an aggressive 3-handed player.

I am in the Small Blind against the Button in a 4-bet pot. The board is relatively dry, and I have top pair with a good kicker. Given the strength of the opponent's 4-bet pre-flop, they have many overpairs in their range that beat me. Betting now would likely only be called by hands that have me beat or cause the opponent to fold their bluffs. Checking allows me to control the pot size, see if the opponent checks behind to take a free card with air, or allows me to re-evaluate their bet sizing if they choose to fire a second barrel. Given my stack size and the pot, playing passively out of position is the most disciplined approach.

I hold an overpair (QQ) on a board of 3d-Js-6s-8h. The pot is currently 8500 and I have 6400 remaining in my stack, making the SPR (Stack-to-Pot Ratio) roughly 0.75. Given the pre-flop 4-bet and the flop action, I am fully committed to this hand. Flashlight's range consists of hands like AJ, KJ, TT, or spade draws. Checking back would allow draws a free card and potentially miss value from Jacks. By shoving all-in, I maximize my value against Jx hands and protect my hand against the flush and straight draws that Flashlight might be holding.

I am facing an all-in situation on the turn after a 4-bet pre-flop. My stack of 8450 leaves me with only 4250 remaining after my previous contributions. Although the opponent's range is strong, my top pair with the Ace of spades as a blocker provides substantial value and prevents me from folding. The pot odds justify calling to see the final card, as folding here against an aggressive opponent in a 3-handed game would be overly cautious. Calling all-in with my remaining stack is the most disciplined and mathematically sound play.

I am in the button position with a very weak hand, Queen-Three offsuit, and I am the short stack with only 2050 chips. There is no reason to commit chips with this holding, as I need to conserve my stack for a more favorable situation or a stronger starting hand to potentially double up. Folding is the most disciplined and mathematically sound decision here.

T3s is too weak to play from the small blind against the big blind, especially when facing the chip leader who can easily apply pressure. Folding and waiting for a better spot is the optimal strategy here.
I am dealt 65o on the Button. This is a weak, disconnected hand with negative expected value to open, even in a 3-handed game. I will fold and conserve my stack for a better spot.

With the Button folding, I am heads-up against the Big Blind from the Small Blind position. Queen-Jack offsuit is a premium hand in this specific positional battle. The Big Blind has a relatively short stack of approximately 19.5 big blinds. I will open with a standard 3x raise to 300 to take control of the pot and put pressure on the short stack. If the Big Blind shoves, QJo often has enough equity to consider a call depending on the exact math, but raising is the standard aggressive play here.

I am in the Big Blind with a weak hand (Queen-Three offsuit) and a short stack (19.5 BB). The opponent (Flash) has been playing very aggressively. Given my limited stack and the poor quality of my cards, calling a raise is not profitable. I should fold and preserve my remaining chips for a better spot.

I am on the button with 9h 5d, which is a very weak hand. While I have a significant chip lead, Flashlight is short-stacked with only 19 big blinds and Pro has a moderate stack. Attempting to steal the blinds with such a weak hand is unnecessarily risky as Flashlight might look to shove and Pro has shown willingness to defend or 3-bet. Folding is the most prudent action.

With only 19 big blinds remaining, my stack is quite short, requiring careful decision-making. Since the Button has folded, I am in a heads-up situation against the Big Blind. While Jack-Seven offsuit is not a premium hand, I am getting good pot odds to call the 50 chip difference to see a flop. Completing the small blind is a standard approach here to potentially outmaneuver the Big Blind or catch a favorable flop.

I'm in the Big Blind with 87o and the Small Blind just limped. 87o is a mediocre offsuit connector, not strong enough to raise for value or as a bluff against a short stack (1850 chips). Checking my option to see a free flop in position is the standard and most profitable play.

The board features an Ace, which is highly likely to have connected with the Big Blind's range. My hand (Jack-Seven) completely missed the flop and holds no showdown value or significant draw. As I am in the small blind, checking is the standard play to see how the opponent acts. Committing chips with such a weak hand on an Ace-high board would be reckless given my short stack.

Flashlight limped pre-flop and checked the flop on an A-8-2 board. I flopped middle pair (eights). A small bet of half the pot (100 chips) is a good play here to extract value from flush draws, straight draws, and smaller pocket pairs, while also denying equity to random overcards that missed the flop. If Flashlight check-raises, considering their short stack, I can comfortably fold knowing I'm likely beaten.

The flop is an Ace-high board (2h, 8d, Ad), which hits the Big Blind's checking range very hard. My hand, Jack-Seven high, has absolutely no showdown value or significant equity against an opponent who is betting. With 98 offsuit in the Big Blind facing a 2.5x open from the Button, folding is the standard and most profitable play. 98o lacks the flush potential to realize equity smoothly out of position, and we don't want to bloat the pot or play a marginal hand against the big stack.

10-5 offsuit is a very weak hand with poor playability, especially given the stack depth of 3300. With two opponents to face and a weak starting hand, the most disciplined and profitable play is to fold and preserve my stack for a better opportunity.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Ten suited. The button folded and the small blind limped in. Since the pot is small and I have a decent hand, checking is the most standard play to see a flop without inflating the pot unnecessarily, especially given my stack size relative to Flash.

I hold top pair with King-Ten on a board of K-J-2-J. On the river, the board is Ks-2h-Js-Jh-Ah. My hand is Kh-3c, giving me two pair: Kings and Jacks with an Ace kicker (K-K-J-J-A). This is a strong hand, but the board is now quite dangerous. I am in the Big Blind with 8-2 offsuit. The action has been a raise from the Button (Flashlight) to 300 and a significantly larger 3-bet from the Small Blind (Pro) to 1950. However, I notice from the tournament status that Pro only has 50 chips remaining after this raise. This means Pro is effectively all-in for 2000 chips. Given that my hand is very weak and there is a lot of action ahead of me, there is no reason to risk a large portion of my stack to call or raise here. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am facing a significant 3-bet from the Small Blind (Pro) after I opened from the Button with AJo. While AJo is a strong hand, a 3-bet in a 3-handed game often indicates strength like AK, AQ, or a pocket pair. Folding is too tight, but a 4-bet could bloat the pot unnecessarily and put me in a tough spot if Pro is playing tight. Calling allows me to see the flop in position, where I can leverage my positional advantage to outplay or reassess based on the board texture, keeping the pot size manageable given my stack.

With only 50 chips remaining and a pot of 4000, I am completely pot-committed. I flopped a gutshot straight draw and have an overcard, but regardless of my hand strength or board texture, shoving my last half big blind is the only mathematically correct play to realize my equity.

The opponent's bet of 50 into a pot of 4050 is extremely small, giving me incredible pot odds. With a gutshot straight draw and an overcard, I am easily priced in to call. Raising at this point would be unnecessary and could isolate me against a range that might have trapped me. Calling is the most rational approach to realize my equity in position.
J3o is a very weak, easily dominated starting hand. With 20.5 big blinds on the Button, the correct play is to fold and wait for a stronger hand to commit chips.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Jack offsuit, which is a very strong hand in three-handed play. The player on the Button (Pro) has already folded. I only need to call 50 more to see a flop, but with a hand this strong, I should be raising to thin the field and build a pot against the Big Blind (Flashlight). Flashlight has a massive stack, but my stack is healthy enough to apply pressure. I will raise to 300.

I am in the big blind with 9-8 offsuit after Flash (small blind) raised to 300. With a pot of 400 and only 200 required to call, the pot odds are favorable. Given that I will be in position post-flop (acting last as the Big Blind), this is a standard defend. I have a healthy stack of 19400, so a small call is a low-risk way to potentially outplay an aggressive opponent.

I raised pre-flop with AJ and was called by the Big Blind. The flop (7-6-10) is a bit coordinated but relatively dry for high-card ranges. As the pre-flop aggressor, I will make a standard continuation bet of 300 (half the pot) to maintain initiative and potentially win the pot immediately. While the board might have connected with some of the Big Blind's defending range, I still have two overcards and the range advantage.

I hold an open-ended straight draw (8 and 9 on a 7-6-T board), which gives me 8 outs. Flash (the small blind) has bet 300 into a 600-chip pot. Given the pot odds of 4:1 and my strong equity with the OESD, calling is the most mathematically sound decision. I am in position, which allows me to evaluate the turn before committing further chips. Raising is a possibility as a semi-bluff, but calling is safer and maintains implied odds should I hit my straight.

The turn card is a Queen, which is an overcard to the flop. While this card is generally better for my range as the pre-flop aggressor, Flashlight called my flop bet, indicating they likely have a piece of the board (a Ten, a Seven, or a Six). With only Ace-high and a Jack, I have some showdown value but not enough to value bet, and a second barrel might be too expensive if they are holding a Ten or better. I will check to control the pot and see how Flashlight responds.

The opponent has checked on the turn, which may indicate weakness or an attempt to trap. I currently hold an open-ended straight draw (needs a 5 or a Jack). Since I am in position and have checked behind, I can take a free card to realize my equity on the river. Betting as a semi-bluff is possible, but checking provides a lower-variance path to potentially hitting my straight.

The river is a 2, which is a complete brick that doesn't change the board state. I currently hold Ace-high. On the turn, I checked and my opponent checked back, which often indicates a hand with some showdown value that doesn't want to bet (like a middle pair) or a draw. Betting as a bluff is risky because the opponent's check on the turn suggests they might be willing to call a small bet with a pair of Tens or Sevens. However, my Ace-high still has a chance to win at showdown against missed straight draws like 8-9 or 5-4. I will check to see the showdown for free or evaluate a small bet if they decide to bluff.

I hold 9-8 high on a 7-6-T-Q-2 board. I have missed all my draws (straight and gutshot potential). Flash has checked twice, suggesting weakness or a hand with low showdown value. While I could attempt a bluff, I am checking behind to reach showdown, as my 9-high is unlikely to win against a pair, and risking more chips as a pure bluff is unnecessary here.

I am on the button with KQs, a very strong hand for 3-handed play. The Pro is extremely short-stacked with only 1950 chips (less than 20 big blinds). I want to put pressure on the blinds or induce a mistake from the short stack. Raising is standard here to take control of the pot and potentially isolate or win the blinds outright.

Jack-four offsuit is a weak hand to defend from the small blind against an aggressive opener. Playing out of position with a marginal hand against a button-raiser is likely to lead to a difficult post-flop situation. Folding is the most disciplined and prudent action to preserve my stack size.

I'm holding an extremely weak offsuit hand (8s 3c) in the Big Blind, facing a 3x raise from the Button. With a short stack of 1950 chips, I need to preserve my stack for a better spot to either shove or defend. This is an easy fold.
My hand (six of spades, nine of clubs) is quite weak, and there is no reason to invest chips to see a flop. Folding is the most prudent decision to preserve my stack for a more favorable hand.

With 19 big blinds remaining and holding an offsuit connector (98o) in the Small Blind, I'm out of position against the Big Blind who easily covers me. It's better to preserve my stack for a more profitable spot rather than limping or raising with a marginal hand.

With 19 big blinds and A9s on the button, I have a strong hand that is well ahead of the blinds' calling ranges. A standard open-raise to 2.5x (250 chips) puts pressure on the opponents while leaving me with enough flexibility to respond to a 3-bet. I am in the Small Blind with 9h 4s. The Button (Pro) has raised to 250. I am in the big blind with Jack-Eight offsuit facing a raise from the button. While J8o is not a premium hand, the pot odds (getting 150 to call into a 400 pot) make it mathematically acceptable to defend my blind. I will call to see a flop and evaluate the situation from there.

I have middle pair on a board of 5-J-9. As the big blind, I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor. Checking allows me to see how Pro approaches this board texture. It is a standard play to check to the aggressor here, and I am prepared to evaluate a continuation bet based on its sizing.

I flopped middle pair with top kicker along with a backdoor flush draw. The board is somewhat coordinated, and while I have a decent made hand, it is vulnerable to overcards. Betting small here (around one-third of the pot) serves to extract value from weaker pairs, pocket pairs, and straight draws, while denying free equity to hands with overcards. With an SPR of 3, a bet of 200 chips provides good protection and keeps the pot under control.

I have top pair with my Jack. Pro's bet of 200 into a 750 pot is small, and given the board texture of 5-9-J, there are few immediate threats. Calling is the most prudent path to keep the pot controlled and see another card without unnecessarily bloating it against a potentially stronger hand like AJ, KJ, or a set.

I am holding top pair with a weak kicker on a board that has now brought a 6c. The board texture is relatively connected with potential straight draws (7-8), and the 6c also brings a flush draw possibility. As the out-of-position player, checking is the most effective way to control the pot size and keep my range disguised. On the turn, I still have middle pair with top kicker, but the board has gotten a bit more coordinated with a club flush draw and some straight draws now possible. I have improved to trips Jacks (J-J-J) on the river, which is a very strong hand given that the turn and river checked through. We only need to be right about 24% of the time to break even, and our Ace kicker ensures we win against any worse 9x. Calling is the mathematically sound choice.

Q2o is a very weak hand with poor playability. While I have the advantage of position on the button, the Big Blind (Pro) is extremely short-stacked with only 900 chips (9 BB). I am in the small blind with a very weak hand (3h, 5d) against a short-stacked big blind (Pro, who has only 9 BBs). With Ace-Nine offsuit on the button and a significant chip lead, raising to 300 is a solid move to pressure the short-stacked blinds and take control of the pot. My position allows me to play aggressively against their likely tighter ranges.

I am dealt Ten-Six offsuit in the Small Blind. This is a very weak starting hand with virtually no playability or high card strength. I am in the big blind with 7c6h. Flashlight, the chip leader with 20,650, has raised to 300 from the button. I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Six offsuit. The Small Blind (Flash) completed the bet to match the Big Blind. There is no raise, and checking is the standard, low-variance play to see a flop with a decent high-card hand without overcommitting in a blind-vs-blind situation.

The board is now paired with the Ten of Hearts, which significantly increases the strength of any hand containing a Ten or a boat/four-of-a-kind possibility. On this board of Ks-Td-Jc-Th-6h, I have two pair, Jacks and Tens. This was a limped pot and has been checked through every street. With only 50 chips left in my stack and a pot of 1700, I am fully committed. I have bottom pair and a gutshot straight draw. Putting the rest of my chips in is the only logical play here.

I am in the Small Blind position with a very weak hand (T2o). However, with a significant stack advantage over Flash, I can afford to play this hand cheaply to see a flop. Completing the small blind by calling the 50 chips is a standard, low-risk approach in this heads-up scenario.

I am in the Big Blind with Qs5s. Flashlight has limped from the Small Blind. I have an underpair (77) on a paired board (KK8). The opponent led out with a bet of 300 into a 600 pot.
